title: Profit Margin Calculator – Margin & Markup
shortDescription: Calculate profit, profit margin, and markup from cost and revenue.
description: Enter your cost and selling price to get the profit, profit margin, and markup percentage. Runs in your browser — no signup.
metaDescription: Free profit margin calculator. Enter cost and revenue to get profit, profit margin %, and markup %. Ideal for pricing products, retail, and e-commerce.

result.empty: Enter cost and revenue to see margin and markup.

sections.heading: Margin vs markup — what's the difference?
paragraphs: Profit is revenue minus cost. Profit margin is profit as a percentage of revenue (profit ÷ revenue), while markup is profit as a percentage of cost (profit ÷ cost). Markup is always higher than margin for the same sale, which is why the two are easy to confuse when pricing.

sections.heading: How to use this calculator
paragraphs: Enter the cost to make or buy the item and the revenue you charge. The calculator returns the profit and both percentages. To hit a target margin, divide cost by (1 − margin); for example, a 40% margin on a $60 cost means a $100 price.

howTo.name: Enter the cost
howTo.text: Type what the product costs you to make or buy.

howTo.name: Enter the revenue
howTo.text: Type the price you sell it for.

howTo.name: Read the results
howTo.text: See profit, profit margin %, and markup %.

faq.q: What is a good profit margin?
faq.a: It varies by industry. Retail often runs 5–20%, software much higher. Compare against peers in your sector rather than a single rule.

faq.q: What's the difference between margin and markup?
faq.a: Margin is profit ÷ revenue; markup is profit ÷ cost. A 50% markup equals a 33% margin.

faq.q: How do I reach a target margin?
faq.a: Divide cost by (1 − target margin). A 40% margin on a $60 cost gives a $100 price.

faq.q: Is this gross or net margin?
faq.a: It calculates gross margin from a single cost and price. Net margin also subtracts overhead, taxes, and other expenses.
Yes. If cost exceeds revenue, profit and margin are negative, meaning the sale loses money.
